content:
# Genesis Chapter 26 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 14v–15r | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_026_page_001.jpg`, `GEN_026_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), 4th century CE — LXX | **View LXX online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0026 (page 22), https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0027 (page 23)

---

## Isaac in the Land of the Philistines

**1** A famine struck the land — not the first one; this was different from the great famine that had come in Abraham's day. Isaac went down to Gerar to see Abimelech, king of the Philistines. **2** There the LORD appeared to him and said, "Do not go down to Egypt. Stay in the land I show you. **3** Live here as a resident alien, and I will be with you. I will bless you — because I am giving all these lands to you and to your descendants, and I will fulfill the oath I swore to your father Abraham. **4** I will make your descendants as countless as the stars in the sky, and I will give them all these lands. Through your descendants, every nation on earth will find blessing in itself — **5** because Abraham listened to me and kept everything I required of him: my commands, my statutes, my instructions, my laws." **6** So Isaac settled in Gerar.

---

## The Same Lie, the Next Generation

**7** When the men of the place asked about Rebekah, Isaac said, "She is my sister." He was afraid to say she was his wife, thinking, "They will kill me over her, because she is beautiful."

**8** But after Isaac had been there a long time, Abimelech king of the Philistines happened to look down through a window and saw Isaac laughing and playing with his wife Rebekah. **9** Abimelech summoned him and said, "So she really is your wife! Why did you tell us she was your sister?"

Isaac answered, "I thought I would be killed over her."

**10** "What have you done to us?" Abimelech said. "One of my men could easily have slept with your wife — and you would have brought guilt down on all of us." **11** So Abimelech issued an order to all his people: "Anyone who touches this man or his wife will be put to death."

---

## Isaac Prospers; the Philistines Grow Resentful

**12** That year Isaac planted crops in that land and harvested a hundredfold. The LORD blessed him, **13** and he grew wealthy — so wealthy, in fact, that he kept growing richer until he was extraordinarily powerful. **14** He accumulated flocks and herds and a large household staff, and the Philistines began to envy him. **15** So they stopped up every well the servants of his father Abraham had dug, filling them in with dirt.

**16** Finally Abimelech said to Isaac, "Move on. You have become too powerful for us."

**17** So Isaac left that area and camped in the valley of Gerar and settled there. **18** He reopened the wells his father Abraham's servants had dug — the Philistines had stopped them up after Abraham died — and he gave them back the same names his father had given them.

---

## The Well Disputes: Esek, Sitnah, Rehoboth

**19** When Isaac's servants dug in the valley and struck a spring of fresh water, **20** the herdsmen of Gerar quarreled with Isaac's herdsmen, claiming, "That water is ours." So Isaac named that well Esek — Dispute — because they had argued over it with him.

**21** His servants dug another well, and they fought over that one too. He named it Sitnah — Hostility.

**22** He moved on from there and dug yet another well. This time no one quarreled over it, so he named it Rehoboth — Room Enough — saying, "Now at last the LORD has made room for us, and we will be fruitful in this land."

---

## God Appears at Beer-sheba

**23** From there Isaac went up to Beer-sheba. **24** That very night the LORD appeared to him and said:

"I am the God of your father Abraham. Do not be afraid — I am with you. I will bless you and multiply your descendants for the sake of my servant Abraham."

**25** Isaac built an altar there and called on the name of the LORD. He pitched his tent there, and his servants began digging a well.

---

## The Covenant with Abimelech

**26** Meanwhile Abimelech came to him from Gerar, along with his adviser Ahuzzath and his army commander Phicol. **27** Isaac said to them, "Why have you come to me? You hate me — you drove me away from you."

**28** They said, "We have clearly seen that the LORD is with you. So we thought: let there be a sworn agreement between us — between you and us. We want to make a covenant with you: **29** that you will do us no harm, just as we never harmed you, treated you only well, and sent you away in peace. Now you are the blessed of the LORD."

**30** So Isaac prepared a feast for them and they ate and drank. **31** Early the next morning they each swore an oath to the other. Then Isaac sent them off, and they left in peace.

**32** That same day Isaac's servants came and told him about the well they had been digging: "We have found water!" **33** He called it Shibah. That is why the city has been called Beer-sheba to this day.

---

## Esau's Hittite Wives

**34** When Esau was forty years old, he married Judith daughter of Beeri the Hittite, and also Basemath daughter of Elon the Hittite. **35** They were a source of bitter grief to Isaac and Rebekah.

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

### Genesis 26 — Isaac's only chapter

Genesis 26 is the only chapter in the entire book of Genesis where Isaac is the protagonist from start to finish. Abraham is the father, Jacob and Esau will be the sons — but Isaac, the middle generation, the child of promise between two great stories, gets precisely one chapter to himself. And in that chapter, he does nearly everything Abraham already did: he faces a famine, he lies about his wife to a foreign king, he digs wells, he strikes a covenant with Abimelech, and God appears to him with the same promises. The chapter is haunted by Abraham's shadow, and the narrative knows it.

---

### Verse 1 — "besides the first famine"

**וַיְהִי רָעָב בָּאָרֶץ מִלְּבַד הָרָעָב הָרִאשׁוֹן אֲשֶׁר הָיָה בִּימֵי אַבְרָהָם** — "And there was a famine in the land, besides the first famine that was in the days of Abraham." This parenthetical narrator's note is doing real work. The first famine drove Abraham down to Egypt (Genesis 12:10), where he also lied about Sarah. The narrator is quietly alerting the careful reader: this chapter is a rerun of that one. But the LORD will intervene before this version plays out the way the Egyptian episode did. The pattern repeats — but with a crucial difference. God speaks first, and the command is: *stay here*. Isaac is not Abraham; he must make Abraham's covenant in his own way, in his own land.

---

### Verse 3 — *ehyeh immekha*

**כִּי-לְךָ וּלְזַרְעֲךָ אֶתֵּן אֶת-כָּל-הָאֲרָצֹת הָאֵל** — "for to you and to your descendants I will give all these lands." The divine speech in vv.3-5 is a formal reiteration of the Abrahamic covenant — land, descendants, blessing to the nations — now transferred to Isaac. But the opening phrase, **כִּי אֶהְיֶה עִמָּךְ** — "for I will be with you" — uses the *ehyeh* verb, the same verb that will give Moses the divine name at Sinai: "I AM who I AM" / "I WILL BE what I WILL BE" (Exodus 3:14). The divine presence promised to Isaac is registered in a word that the Pentateuch will return to at its most theologically charged moment. Isaac is not told only that God will bless him. He is told that God will *be present with him* — and that presence is the ground of everything else.

---

### Verse 5 — the law before the law

**עֵקֶב אֲשֶׁר-שָׁמַע אַבְרָהָם בְּקֹלִי וַיִּשְׁמֹר מִשְׁמַרְתִּי מִצְוֹתַי חֻקּוֹתַי וְתוֹרֹתָי** — "because Abraham listened to my voice and kept my charge, my commandments, my statutes, and my laws." This verse lands in the reader's ear with the full weight of Sinai — the vocabulary of *mitzvot*, *chuqqot*, *torot* is covenant legal language, the language of the Torah-giving to Moses. Yet it is said here, about Abraham, centuries before Sinai. The rabbis debated what exactly Abraham kept — some said he kept the whole Torah in advance, intuitively, by the spirit. The narrative does not answer that question; it simply insists that Abraham's obedience is the moral ground on which Isaac's blessing rests. The promise flows through faithfulness. The covenant is not automatic.

---

### Verse 8 — *metsacheq*: the wordplay the Hebrew demands

**וַיַּשְׁקֵף אֲבִימֶלֶךְ מֶלֶךְ פְּלִשְׁתִּים בְּעַד הַחַלּוֹן וַיַּרְא וְהִנֵּה יִצְחָק מְצַחֵק אֵת רִבְקָה אִשְׁתּוֹ** — "And Abimelech looked through the window and saw — and there was Isaac *metsacheq* with Rebekah his wife." The verb **מְצַחֵק** (*metsacheq*) — "laughing, playing, caressing" — is the Piel participle of *tsachaq*, the root from which Isaac's own name, *Yitschaq*, is derived. Abimelech sees Isaac doing the thing that names him. The name "Isaac" means "he laughs/plays" — and here he is, laughing and playing with his wife, and it is precisely this act, this name-act, that exposes his lie. The Hebrew reader catches the irony immediately. I have rendered the verb as "laughing and playing" — trying to preserve the intimacy of the scene while staying close to the lexical range of *tsachaq*, which does not necessarily imply anything sexual but does imply tender, playful affection that no sister-brother relationship would ordinarily include.

---

### Verse 10 — the weight of potential guilt

**וַיֹּאמֶר אֲבִימֶלֶךְ מַה-זֹּאת עָשִׂיתָ לָּנוּ כִּמְעַט שָׁכַב אַחַד הָעָם אֶת-אִשְׁתֶּךָ וְהֵבֵאתָ עָלֵינוּ אָשָׁם** — "And Abimelech said: What is this you have done to us? One of the people could nearly have lain with your wife, and you would have brought *asham* upon us." The word **אָשָׁם** (*asham*) means guilt-incurred-by-action, the kind of guilt that requires a reparation offering (the guilt-offering in Leviticus is also called *asham*). This is not mere embarrassment or social awkwardness; it is moral and cultic liability — the kind that falls on a community. Abimelech is not merely annoyed. He is recognizing that Isaac's deception very nearly made his entire people ritually and morally guilty of violation. The Philistine king understands the gravity of covenant faithfulness, even when the patriarch does not behave well. This is one of the recurring ironies of the wife-sister narratives: the foreigner often behaves more honorably than the patriarch.

---

### Verses 18-22 — the well theology: Esek, Sitnah, Rehoboth

The three wells of Genesis 26 are not just geography — they are a theology of persistence. Isaac digs, loses the well to quarrel (*Esek*, verse 20), digs again, loses again to hostility (*Sitnah*, verse 21), and digs a third time — and this time there is room (*Rehoboth*, verse 22). The names trace a movement: dispute, then antagonism, then finally space. The Hebrew names are themselves a small narrative: **עֵשֶׂק** (*Esek*) — strife, contention; **שִׂטְנָה** (*Sitnah*) — opposition, hostility (the root of *Satan*, the adversary); **רְחֹבוֹת** (*Rehoboth*) — broad places, room, open space.

Isaac's commentary on Rehoboth — **פָּרָה בָאָרֶץ** ("we will be fruitful in the land") — echoes the creation blessing (*peru ur'vu*, "be fruitful and multiply," Genesis 1:28). The persistence through conflict is not merely personal stubbornness; it is covenantal tenacity. Isaac keeps digging because he has been promised the land. The wells are acts of faith as much as acts of hydraulics.

---

### Verse 24 — "Do not be afraid"

**אַל-תִּירָא כִּי-אִתְּךָ אָנֹכִי** — "Do not fear, for I am with you." This is the first divine appearance to Isaac by night — the LORD appears *that very night* after Isaac arrives at Beer-sheba. The divine speech is brief: I am Abraham's God; do not be afraid; I will bless you; your descendants will multiply. The *lo tira* ("do not fear") formula is a characteristic marker of a genuine divine encounter in the patriarchal narratives. God shows up in the dark, when the man has just arrived somewhere unfamiliar and is, presumably, uncertain of his footing. The divine word precedes the covenant meal, the oath, the well, everything else. The chapter's inner logic is: God appears first, blessing follows, and Abimelech shows up to ratify what he has already observed God doing.

---

### Verse 28 — "we have clearly seen"

**רָאוֹ רָאִינוּ כִּי-הָיָה יְהוָה עִמָּךְ** — "Seeing we have seen that the LORD is with you." The infinitive absolute *ra'o ra'inu* is an emphatic construction in Hebrew: "we have surely seen," "we can plainly see," "the evidence is unmistakable." Abimelech does not come because he likes Isaac or because diplomacy requires it. He comes because what has happened to Isaac in Abimelech's own territory is so obviously blessed that the Philistine king recognizes it as divine and wants to secure good terms with the man God is backing. The enemy who expelled Isaac now returns to negotiate — not from weakness but from clear-eyed recognition of where power actually resides.

---

### Verse 33 — Beer-sheba's second etymology

**וַיִּקְרָא אֹתָהּ שִׁבְעָה עַל-כֵּן שֵׁם-הָעִיר בְּאֵר שֶׁבַע עַד הַיּוֹם הַזֶּה** — "And he called it Shibah; therefore the name of the city is Beer-sheba to this day." The Hebrew *shiv'ah* means "seven" or "oath" — the same double meaning that generates Beer-sheba's first etymology in Genesis 21:31, where Abraham and Abimelech make their own covenant. Seven ewes were set apart to seal the oath; hence Beer-sheba, "Well of Seven" or "Well of the Oath." Now Isaac digs a well at Beer-sheba and gives it the same name, his own form of the etymology. Father and son, at the same sacred geography, establishing the same covenantal relationship with the same Philistine dynasty — the pattern is complete. Beer-sheba is the city that gets named twice, by both patriarchs, for the same reason: because this is where the blessed ones make binding agreements, and water flows from the ground when they do.

---

### Verses 34-35 — Esau's Hittite wives as shadow over the blessing

**וַיִּהְיוּ מֹרַת רוּחַ לְיִצְחָק וּלְרִבְקָה** — "And they were bitterness of spirit to Isaac and to Rebekah." The phrase **מֹרַת רוּחַ** (*morat ruach*) is vivid and rare — literally "bitterness of breath/spirit," describing not just sadness but an embittered, grievous feeling that affects something as intimate as breath. Esau is forty years old, the age at which his father Isaac married (Genesis 25:20). But where Isaac brought a single woman, Rebekah, from the right family — the Aramean family of Abraham's brother — Esau has married two Hittite women, women from the foreign population of Canaan. The contrast is pointed. The chapter ends not with a celebration of the covenant but with a cloud over the next generation. Esau's marriages will be the direct trigger for Genesis 27, where Rebekah moves to secure the blessing for Jacob before Esau can carry it out of the family line.

The LXX renders *morat ruach* as **ἐρίζουσαι** ("quarreling") — focusing on outward conflict between the wives and the household — but the Hebrew is more inward and more devastating: they caused the *spirit* of Isaac and Rebekah to go bitter. The grief is not merely domestic inconvenience; it is the grief of people who can see the covenant blessing moving in a wrong direction.

---

### The structure of Genesis 26 as a whole

The chapter unfolds in a triple movement:

1. **The covenant reiterated** (vv.1-6): God appears; the Abrahamic promises are transferred to Isaac; he is commanded to stay.

2. **Isaac in Gerar** (vv.7-22): He fails morally (the wife-sister lie) and is exposed by a pagan king, yet God prospers him anyway. He digs wells, loses them to conflict, persists, and eventually finds room.

3. **The covenant confirmed** (vv.23-33): God appears again; Isaac worships; Abimelech comes to ratify what God has done; the covenant is sealed at the place whose name encodes covenant.

The frame around this entire chapter is the Abrahamic covenant — reiterated at the beginning (vv.3-5) and sealed again at the end (vv.28-31). Isaac is not Abraham. He is more passive, more domestic, less heroic. He does not argue with God; he does not bargain over cities; he does not volunteer to sacrifice. He digs wells and stays where he is told to stay. And God blesses him extravagantly anyway. The chapter suggests that faithfulness — modest, persistent, ordinary faithfulness — is itself a form of covenant-keeping. You do not have to be Abraham. You have to be Isaac.

---

*Translated from the Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 14v–15r, photographed by the West Semitic Research Project (Bruce E. Zuckerman). Genesis 26 begins in the right column of folio 14v (page 001 for this chapter) following the end of Genesis 25, and continues through folio 15r (page 002), concluding at verse 35 before Genesis 27 begins. Secondary reference: Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Biblioteca Apostolica Vaticana, pages 22–23 (accessible at https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0026 and /0027). The Vaticanus LXX follows the Hebrew closely throughout Genesis 26. Notable LXX variations: verse 8 — the LXX renders *metsacheq* as **παίζοντα** ("playing"), losing the Isaac-name wordplay that the Hebrew embeds in the verb; verse 10 — the LXX renders *asham* as **ἁμαρτίαν** ("sin") rather than the more precise cultic term "guilt-offering liability"; verses 20-22 — the LXX preserves the three well names (Αδικία, Ἐχθρία, Εὐρυχωρία) in rough Greek equivalents; verse 28 — the LXX renders the emphatic infinitive absolute *ra'o ra'inu* as **ἑωράκαμεν** (simple perfect), smoothing the Hebrew emphasis; verse 33 — the LXX reads **Ὅρκος** ("oath") for *Shibah*, taking the "oath" branch of the ambiguous etymology. No DSS fragments of Genesis 26 are known from the Qumran scrolls.*

---

*— PNEUMA*
